tier_intro: This section is completely optional. It helps AI adapt to how YOUR brain actually works — not how textbooks say brains should work. No diagnosis needed. No judgment. We're asking about experiences and adaptations, not labels. Skip anything that doesn't feel relevant.

tier_complete.headline: Brain OS profile saved.
tier_complete.body: Most AI tools assume everyone's brain works the same way. Yours now doesn't. AI will adapt pacing, structure, reminders, and task framing to how you actually function — not the neurotypical default. This is one of the highest-impact sections in the whole profile.

questions.question: When you sit down to start something important, your brain...

options.label: Just starts — I sit down, I begin, no drama
Needs a warm-up — coffee, music, clearing desk, a small easy task first
Procrastinates until pressure kicks in, then hyperfocuses at the last minute
Often freezes — knows what to do but can't make the first move
Completely depends — easy if it's interesting, nearly impossible if it's not

questions.meta_profiling: Response speed and length here are secondary signals. Paralysis/deadline patterns are strong ADHD indicators. 'Depends on interest' = interest-based nervous system.
questions.why_this_matters: AI can adjust how it frames task starts — breaking them into smaller entry points or adding urgency framing based on what actually gets you moving.

questions.question: What actually helps you start when you're stuck?

options.label: Someone else in the room (or on video) working alongside me
Setting a timer — even 5 minutes
Telling someone what I'm about to do
Breaking it to the smallest possible first action
Changing location (cafe, different room)
Specific music or background noise
Honestly, nothing reliable — it's random

questions.meta_profiling: Which adaptations they've already discovered tells you about their self-awareness and coping sophistication.
questions.why_this_matters: AI can actively suggest or simulate these conditions when helping you start tasks.

questions.question: You think 'I'll just do this quick thing — 10 minutes max.' You look up and it's been...

options.label: About what I expected — my time estimates are usually close
A bit longer, but not drastically — maybe 1.5x what I thought
45 minutes to 2 hours — time genuinely disappears
I genuinely have no idea until I check my phone — could be 10 min or 3 hours

questions.meta_profiling: Time blindness is one of the most impactful ADHD traits for AI assistance. Note their certainty — people with time blindness often phrase this with humor or resignation.
questions.why_this_matters: Time blindness changes how AI should structure task estimates, reminders, and planning — it's not about discipline, it's about perception.

questions.question: Which tools or strategies actually help you with time?

options.label: Visual timers (Time Timer, phone timer visible on screen)
Multiple calendar alarms — including 'start getting ready' alerts
Anchoring to fixed events ('after lunch', 'before 3pm call')
Pomodoro or fixed work/break intervals
External deadlines from other people — self-imposed ones don't work
Nothing reliable — I'm just always late or surprised

questions.meta_profiling: Check if they mention external deadlines specifically — strong signal that self-regulation is insufficient alone.
questions.why_this_matters: AI can proactively use the strategies that actually work for you.

questions.question: When something REALLY grabs your attention — a project, a topic, a problem...

options.label: I don't really experience this — I stay pretty consistent regardless of interest
I get into flow states, but I can exit when needed
I go deep and lose track of time, but external interruptions can pull me out
I fully disappear — hours pass, I forget to eat, interruptions feel physically jarring

questions.meta_profiling: Intensity of description is itself a signal — people with strong hyperfocus often describe it vividly or with a mix of pride and frustration.
questions.why_this_matters: Hyperfocus is a superpower and a liability. AI can help harness it strategically and protect against its costs.

questions.question: After a hyperfocus session, you typically feel...

options.label: Energized — that was productive and satisfying
Slightly drained — need a break but recovered quickly
Wiped out — rest of day is often gone
Disoriented — takes a while to re-enter normal life

questions.meta_profiling: Post-hyperfocus crash pattern informs how AI should structure recovery time in planning.
questions.why_this_matters: Knowing the recovery cost helps AI plan what to schedule after deep work sessions.

questions.question: You're doing a boring but necessary task. Your brain starts wanting to...

options.label: Check phone — notifications, social, anything
Open a new tab 'just quickly'
Get a snack or drink — suddenly very hungry
Reorganize or clean something nearby
Just... drift off into thinking about something else entirely
Put on music or a podcast to make it bearable
Push through — I have decent tolerance for boring work

questions.meta_profiling: Multiple selections = higher dopamine-seeking tendency. Phone + new tab + snack = strong ADHD pattern. Note if they selected 'push through' alongside others — might be masking.
questions.why_this_matters: AI can gamify, chunk, or pair boring tasks with stimulation based on your specific patterns.

questions.question: Your ideal work environment sound level is...

spectrum.min_label: Complete silence — any noise breaks concentration
spectrum.max_label: Loud and busy — I focus better in a cafe or with music
spectrum.mid_label: Low background noise or ambient sounds

questions.meta_profiling: Extremes on either end can indicate sensory processing differences. Note this against their task initiation style.
questions.why_this_matters: Affects environment recommendations, focus music suggestions, and whether to recommend background noise tools.

questions.question: Someone gives you a 4-step verbal instruction. You...

options.label: Remember all 4 steps easily and execute them
Remember the first and last steps, fuzzy on the middle
Immediately write it down — I know I'll lose it otherwise
Retain about 2 steps before it starts dropping off
Ask them to slow down or repeat — verbal instructions don't stick

questions.meta_profiling: Working memory is one of the most consistently impacted areas in ADHD. The 'compensated' response shows strong self-awareness.
questions.why_this_matters: AI adjusts how it delivers multi-step instructions — chunking, numbered lists, or asking you to confirm each step.

questions.question: You're mid-task when someone interrupts you. Getting back to where you were feels like...

options.label: Easy — I pick right back up
Takes a moment to reload — maybe 2-5 minutes to get back
The thread is partially lost — I usually have to re-read what I was doing
The task is basically dead — I rarely return to it after interruption

questions.meta_profiling: Interruption sensitivity is separate from introversion. Someone who gets frustrated at interruptions may be protecting fragile focus, not being antisocial.
questions.why_this_matters: AI can build in 'do not interrupt' framing and help you use context notes to resume interrupted tasks.

questions.question: What helps you protect focus from interruptions?

options.label: Phone/notifications off — no incoming signals
Headphones on (even without music) — visual signal to others
Being somewhere people don't expect me (different room, cafe)
Scheduled 'no meeting' blocks communicated to others
Writing where I am before I get interrupted so I can return

questions.meta_profiling: Practical coping strategies indicate someone who has had to figure this out the hard way.
questions.why_this_matters: AI can suggest these strategies proactively when you start deep work sessions.

questions.question: Someone doesn't respond to your message for 3 hours. You...

options.label: Don't notice or think about it until I see the reply
Mildly wonder what's up, but it doesn't stick
Start replaying the message — did I say something wrong?
Assume they're upset with me or I messed up — strong gut feeling

questions.meta_profiling: Rejection Sensitive Dysphoria is common with ADHD. The intensity of negative assumption vs reality is the key signal. Note if they add context or excuses in their answer.
questions.why_this_matters: AI can frame feedback carefully and avoid phrasing that could be misread as rejection or disapproval.

questions.question: When you get critical feedback, even constructive, your first physical reaction is often...

options.label: A gut drop — physical feeling of dread or shame before I even process the content
Flush of defensiveness — my first instinct is to explain or justify
Shut down — I need time before I can engage with it
Fine actually — the first answer was about silence, not criticism specifically

questions.meta_profiling: Physical/somatic responses to feedback indicate RSD specifically, not just general sensitivity.
questions.why_this_matters: AI adjusts critique delivery — leading with what works before what doesn't, using 'I noticed' framing over 'you did wrong'.

questions.question: Around new people or in professional settings, how much do you filter your natural behavior?

spectrum.min_label: I'm the same everywhere — what you see is what you get
spectrum.max_label: I perform a 'professional version' — exhausting but feels necessary

questions.meta_profiling: High masking scores combined with other neurodivergent indicators = likely masking ADHD or autism. Exhaustion from masking is a real cost AI should acknowledge.
questions.why_this_matters: AI can be a place where you don't have to mask — knowing this, it won't police your informal language or assume you need professional tone.

questions.question: When something unexpected ruins your plan — your calendar changes, a task goes sideways, someone cancels — your gut reaction is...

options.label: Adapt easily — I pivot and figure it out
Briefly annoyed, recover within 30 minutes
Disrupted for a few hours — the day feels 'ruined' even if logically it isn't
Full emotional reaction that's hard to control — intensity feels disproportionate and I know it

questions.meta_profiling: The qualifier 'I know it' in the last option is important — it signals self-awareness about disproportionality, which is common in ADHD emotional dysregulation.
questions.why_this_matters: AI can build in 'disruption buffers' in planning and avoid scheduling back-to-back critical tasks where one failure cascades.

questions.question: What helps you recover when emotions are running hot?

options.label: Physical movement — walk, exercise, anything that burns it off
Being alone until it passes — no input
Talking to someone (or writing it out)
Sleep or rest — it's usually better the next day
Total distraction — TV, game, something absorbing
Nothing consistent — I just have to wait it out

questions.meta_profiling: Regulation strategies reveal how developed their coping toolkit is.
questions.why_this_matters: AI can suggest these strategies proactively when it detects stress signals in conversation.

questions.question: When you're thinking hard, excited, or deep in concentration — your body tends to...

options.label: Bounce or tap my leg
Fidget with something — pen, ring, phone
Pace or need to move around
Make sounds — hum, click, mutter to myself
Stay pretty still — no noticeable movement patterns
I'm not usually aware of what my body is doing

questions.meta_profiling: Stimming awareness is a direct indicator of sensory/nervous system difference. Note if they express any shame in their response.
questions.why_this_matters: Confirms sensory processing patterns and helps AI understand that movement-friendly work setups may be beneficial.

questions.question: Topics or domains where you can talk for hours without getting bored — list them (or describe)
questions.placeholder: E.g. 'mechanical keyboards, Roman history, fermentation, Rust programming, urban planning...'
questions.meta_profiling: The specificity and depth of their answer reveals how developed their special interests are. Multiple specific topics vs one deep one vs vague answers all differ meaningfully.
questions.why_this_matters: AI can use these as engagement hooks, learning metaphors, and dopamine boosters when you need motivation for other tasks.

questions.question: Your natural sleep schedule, if you had zero obligations, would be...

options.label: Early to bed, early to rise — I'm naturally a morning person
Roughly conventional — 11pm-7am feels natural
Night owl — I come alive at 11pm and hate mornings
Extreme — I'd naturally sleep 3am-11am if I could
No consistent pattern — it shifts around

questions.meta_profiling: Delayed sleep phase is more common in ADHD. Irregular pattern can indicate either ADHD or high stress.
questions.why_this_matters: AI adapts scheduling recommendations and peak-hour suggestions to your actual biology, not societal defaults.

questions.question: Which of these actually help YOU work better? Select everything that's genuinely useful.

options.label: Body doubling — someone present (physical or virtual)
Visible countdown timers during work
Specific music (lo-fi, binaural beats, brown noise)
Scheduled movement breaks every 20-30 min
Seeing progress visually — task lists with checkboxes, progress bars
Hard external deadlines (self-imposed don't work)
Novelty and variety — switching between tasks keeps me engaged
Being given ONE thing to focus on — too many options paralyzes
Scripted 'getting started' rituals that remove decisions

questions.meta_profiling: This is the direct adaptation inventory — selections here directly configure AI behavior. Note any contradictions with previous answers (e.g., novelty + single priority both selected = context-dependent).
questions.why_this_matters: These become the actual settings AI uses to structure how it helps you — directly configures output style and interaction patterns.

questions.question: Right now, your energy reserves feel like...

options.label: Full — I feel like myself, motivated, able to take on things
Running on fumes — I'm functional but everything takes more effort
Depleted — I'm pushing through but not sustainably
Burnt out — basic things feel huge, I've lost the 'want to'

questions.meta_profiling: Burnout self-awareness is important. Note if they answer matter-of-factly vs with deflection. High burnout + high masking = significant risk.
questions.why_this_matters: AI adjusts ambition level, task complexity, and check-in frequency based on your current reserves — not an idealized version of you.

questions.question: Is this recent, or has it been the background state for a while?

options.label: Recent — something specific happened
A few months
Honestly, a long time — this is just how things are

questions.meta_profiling: Chronic burnout requires AI to actively push back on overcommitment, not just accommodate it.
questions.why_this_matters: Chronic burnout changes the whole approach — AI prioritizes recovery and sustainability over productivity.

questions.question: Anything else about how your brain works that you'd want AI to know? No need for labels — just describe.
questions.placeholder: E.g. 'I need instructions in writing, not spoken', 'I work much better at night', 'sensory stuff with certain textures', 'I'm autistic and prefer direct communication', anything...
questions.meta_profiling: Open text here catches everything the structured questions miss. Also reveals self-narrative and identity around neurodivergence — matter-of-fact vs careful framing says a lot.
questions.why_this_matters: You know your brain better than any question bank does. This is your override field.
